---
title: "Chapter 4: Placer-Au prospectivity (northern Sierra)"
subtitle: "Tertiary deep-gravel and Quaternary modern-channel calibrated probability rasters fused at 250 m on the canonical California placer province"
sidebar: placer
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## What this is

A 250 m calibrated probability raster for placer-Au prospectivity across the
northern Sierra deep-gravel belt, built as two per-population classifiers
(Tertiary buried paleo-channels, Quaternary modern-channel alluvium) fused
into a single GeoTIFF that the [goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/)
live tool samples per claim. The geological setting is Lindgren 1911 (USGS
Professional Paper 73) at canonical resolution: hydraulic-mined deep gravels
from Malakoff Diggins south through Forest Hill, plus the modern Yuba, Bear,
and American river placers below them.

Placer-Au modeling sits about a decade behind lode in the published
literature. The lode side has stacked supervised pipelines with spatial-block
cross-validation, leakage audits, and external blind tests as standard
practice. The placer side mostly does not, because the data is sparse (point
occurrences with inconsistent free-text deposit-type descriptions), the positives concentrate
in a few well-known districts, and the geomorphic chain from lode source to
placer trap involves transport mechanics that don't show up in a static
feature stack. The only production-grade transferable placer model in the
published record is the USGS Alaska watershed scorer used in the Cross,
Frost, and Karl assessments, and even that is a knowledge-driven weighted
index rather than a supervised classifier. This chapter ports the Alaska
scorer to the northern Sierra at 250 m, then layers a supervised pass on
top with separate classifiers for the Tertiary deep-gravel and Quaternary
modern-channel populations, fused per-cell into a single calibrated
probability raster. The deliverable feeds Chapter 3 (goldbug); the sibling
Cape Nome property in Chapter 1 (Bear Cub) is different geology and a
different audience but shares the population-level reasoning.

Two caveats to surface up front so the headline numbers below are
read correctly. First, the v3.6 Tertiary out-of-fold AUC of 0.993 and
anchor-capture AUC-PA of 0.998 include 7 to 15 percentage points of
polygon-rasterization neighbor-cell inflation. The Tertiary positive
labels are the 158 Orlando hydraulic-pit polygons rasterized to the
250 m grid (~2,700 weighted cells); cells immediately adjacent to a
positive inherit the same feature values, and the model learns that
neighborhood. The leak-free Tertiary AUC sits in the 0.85 to 0.97
range. The Quaternary AUC-PA of 0.840 is not affected by this
mechanism and is the population-level transfer-honest number. Second,
this is a northern-Sierra-only model. The training labels concentrate
north of 39° latitude. Cells in the southern Mother Lode (Tuolumne,
Mariposa, Calaveras) score near the raster floor because the model
has not seen southern Quaternary signal. The v3.7.0 retrain
expanding the Quaternary classifier to the full Mother Lode is
queued.

## What got built

**Phase 1 knowledge-driven index.** I ported the USGS Alaska watershed scorer
to the northern Sierra at 250 m grid resolution and added Sierra-specific
features for deep-gravel geometry (hillside benches above modern drainage,
not channel bottoms). Eleven features combine into a p1/p99-clipped
min-max-normalized weighted sum: `distance_to_lode_m`,
`paleochannel_likelihood` (a composite of NHDPlus HR REM, local relief, and
geomorphon GMI), `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m`, `tertiary_terrace_likelihood`
(TPI-high ∧ slope-low ∧ not-Qa geometric mean), `catchment_au_hawkes` (Hawkes
1976 dual-decay catchment aggregation of NGDB + NURE stream-sediment Au), and
six others. Seven historic hydraulic-mining districts (Malakoff Diggins,
North San Juan, Dutch Flat, You Bet, Forest Hill, Iowa Hill, Michigan Bluff)
define the validation gate: each must land in the top decile of the score,
and none appears in the training label set. The Phase 1 model gets 7 of 7. A
held-out Lindgren PP73 secondary set of 13 Tertiary-named districts
(Smartsville, Camptonville, Alleghany, Damascus, and nine others) lands 11
of 13 in the top quintile at median decile 0.

**Phase 2 supervised, per population.** I trained one classifier stack per
geomorphic placer population. Three base learners run under 20 km
spatial-block cross-validation with per-fold Hawkes catchment-geochem
recompute: Random Forest, LightGBM, XGBoost. A logistic-regression
meta-learner combines the spatial-block out-of-fold predictions. Isotonic
calibration via `CalibratedClassifierCV` produces the final probability
scale. The two populations train on disjoint feature stacks (17 features for
Tertiary, 22 for Quaternary) and fuse per-cell via `np.maximum` into the
deliverable raster. Quaternary uses the Kiryo et al. 2017 non-negative PU
loss (`nnPU`) instead of the Mordelet-Vert PU bagging the Tertiary side
uses, because the 0.054% Quaternary positive rate sits inside nnPU's design
range. Anchor districts and Lindgren secondary cells are masked out of
training (label-side and pseudo-negative-side) so the validation gates stay
real held-out tests.

**Leakage-resistant feature stack.** v2 had `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` as a
raw distance feature, and the v2 Random Forest used it as a label-inverse:
positive labels are pit polygon centroids, so the distance-to-pit feature at
zero IS the label. v3 sets the feature to NaN within 1 km of any pit polygon
so the model cannot trivially identify positives by zero-distance lookup.
Distance-to-lode and distance-to-fault features switched from v2's hard 30
km caps to Gaussian falloffs (σ = 12 km and 3 km, respectively), so
districts like Forest Hill at 12 km from the nearest mapped lode source get
a non-zero score instead of v2's hard zero. MRDS placer records
reclassified per CGS 2010 PTYPE plus 500 m pit-proximity: 136 records that
v2 assigned to Quaternary moved to Tertiary because they sit on Tertiary
auriferous-gravel mapped units within pit-proximity range. Final v3 positive
counts: 292 Tertiary cells and 437 Quaternary cells across an 805,294-cell
AOI.

**Held-out validation.** The Lindgren PP73 secondary set is the diagnostic
that distinguishes v2 (leakage) from v3 (signal). Eleven Tertiary-named
secondary districts land at 81.8% in the top quintile of the v3 Tertiary
calibrated raster (median decile 1), all 500 m de-duped from any training
pit. v2 put 8 of those 13 in the zero bin. Anchor districts hit 7 of 7 in
the top decile of the v3 raster, with capture@1% of 71.4% (5 of 7 anchors in
the top 1% of cells, enrichment 71x) and capture@5% of 85.7%. Calibration
ECE measured pre-isotonic at 0.0002 and post-isotonic at 0.0002; the input
stack arrives well-calibrated and isotonic doesn't have room to move it.

![The v3 Tertiary calibrated probability raster, displayed as percentile rank across the AOI because the calibrated probability has a long zero tail that linear color stretching would wash out. Cyan outlines are the 158 Orlando 2016 hydraulic-mine pit polygons that supplied the training labels. Red dots mark the seven anchor districts, none of which appear in the training labels; all seven land in the top decile. The hot zone traces the Mother Lode trend through the western half of the AOI; the eastern half toward the Sierra crest is mostly zero-probability black.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_calibrated_tertiary_map.png)

**Quaternary v3.** The Quaternary classifier finished its full pipeline on
2026-06-05 with a stacking out-of-fold AUC of 0.832 across 50 spatial
blocks (RF base mean 0.601, LightGBM 0.570, XGBoost 0.708; stacking
moves the result +12 pp above the best base learner). PR-AUC is 0.004;
the 0.054% positive rate puts a hard floor on precision-weighted area
regardless of ranking quality. The five
Lindgren PP73 Quaternary-named secondary districts (Downieville,
Goodyears Bar, Sierra City, Brandy City, Camptonville) land at 80% in
the top quintile of the Quaternary calibrated raster (4 of 5; median
decile 0), clearing the ship gate of 30% by 50 pp.

![The v3 Quaternary calibrated probability raster, percentile-rank stretch with viridis colormap. NHD flowlines are overlaid in dark blue because modern-channel placers concentrate along streams. The five Lindgren PP73 Quaternary-named held-out districts are marked as orange dots. The active drainage network is what the Quaternary model is ranking, not the Tertiary deep-gravel belt the Tertiary chapter section shows.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_calibrated_quaternary_map.png)

**Quaternary is materially harder than Tertiary at this resolution.**
Tertiary stack OOF is 0.970; Quaternary is 0.832. The gap is real and
structural. Tertiary placer targets are hydraulic-mining-pit polygons that
average 50-500 cells at 250 m, with broad geomorphic signatures
(paleo-channel REM, terrace TPI, lithology, pit-buffered distance) the
model can learn directly. Quaternary placer targets are MRDS placer
records (point occurrences with median 555 m positional offset per a
v3.5 recon) sitting in modern stream channels 10-50 m wide. The features
that should encode the Quaternary physics (`spi_band`, `ksn`,
`distance_downstream_from_lode_m`, `paleochannel_likelihood`) are
computed from a 250 m DEM that smears them across the channel + gravel
bar + valley wall. SHAP on the Quaternary fullfit confirms the model
fell back on coarse-scale features that do carry signal at 250 m
(`elevation`, `distance_to_lode_m`, `tpi`, `nearest_reach_slope`,
`gravity`); the channel-physics features rank 11-18. The recipe-match
gate at 1 of 5 expected features is a fail in the ship-gate sense but
not a leakage signal. No `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` in the Q
top-5, no features cross the 0.10 leakage-correlation threshold. The
model is doing the right thing at the wrong resolution.

![v3 Tertiary vs Quaternary AUC by base learner and stack. Tertiary climbs cleanly across all three base learners (RF 0.749, LGBM 0.637, XGB 0.826) into a stack OOF of 0.970. Quaternary is lower across the board (RF 0.601, LGBM 0.570, XGB 0.708) and the stack reaches 0.832. That sits well above any single Q base learner because the meta combines complementary errors, but still meaningfully below the Tertiary number.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_t_vs_q_auc_comparison.png)

![Per-fold ROC-AUC for the v3 Quaternary spatial-block CV (50 folds). Each dot is one block's held-out AUC for that base learner; the bar is the per-model mean. Stacking OOF is plotted as the black bar at 0.832. The high per-fold spread (LGBM ranges 0.20-0.93 across folds) reflects the difficulty of localizing 437 positives across 50 spatial blocks; some blocks have only 1-2 positives where the AUC is statistically noisy.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_q_per_fold_auc.png)

![Lindgren PP73 Quaternary-named held-out districts in the v3 Quaternary calibrated raster (n=5). Four of five land in the top quintile (deciles 0 or 1); only Sierra City sits at decile 4. This is the held-out test that distinguishes the Quaternary model from v2's killed-mid-stacking baseline (where Q had no calibrated raster); the v3 plan's ship gate is ≥30% top quintile, v3 delivers 80%.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_lindgren_q_decile_histogram.png)

**Fused deliverable.** The Tertiary and Quaternary calibrated rasters fuse
per-cell via `np.maximum` into a single GeoTIFF. The fused raster passes
the anchor ship gate at 7/7 in the top decile because the seven anchors
are Tertiary hydraulic-mining sites and Tertiary scores dominate at those
cells. The fused Phase 2 P-A AUC is 1.000 against the anchor set; Phase
2 Tertiary-alone P-A AUC is 0.979; Phase 2 Quaternary-alone P-A AUC is
0.840. The Quaternary contribution to the fused raster is at cells in
active stream alluvium where Tertiary returns near-zero, which is exactly
where modern-channel placers should rank above the deep-gravel signal.

## What we learned

**v1: a fixed weighted index is the diagnostic, not the model.** Phase 1
puts 7 of 7 anchors in the top decile, but the eleven weights are
geologist-set rather than learned from labels. Phase 1 is the held-out gate
v2 and v3 have to clear; it isn't the deliverable. It also fails to
specialize. Phase 1 runs a single Tertiary-tuned scorer across both
populations because all seven training anchors are Tertiary, but Quaternary
modern-channel placers need stream power, catchment Hawkes, and
`is_quaternary_alluvium` weighted differently. v2 and v3 fix this with
per-population classifiers.

**v2: the headline number was a leakage artifact.** v2 Tertiary RF averaged
0.995 across 14 spatial blocks with standard deviation 0.008. The model was
reading `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` as a label-inverse: pit polygon
centroids ARE the positive labels, and a Random Forest with deep trees and
no shrinkage on small leaves trivially identifies the cells at zero
distance. The stacking meta-learner pulled the headline back to 0.961
because LightGBM resisted the shortcut, but the Lindgren held-out result
told the real story. Five of the 13 Tertiary secondary districts landed in
the d1 cluster matching the anchors; eight landed in the d9 zero bin. That
is not the gate passing. It is the model declining to rank the held-out
cells above the floor because the shortcut feature has nothing to anchor
their probability to.

![Of the 13 Tertiary-named Lindgren PP73 secondary districts I hand-geocoded, the 500 m dedup buffer against trained positives drops Alleghany and Indian Hill, leaving 11 that v2 and v3 both score. The v2 calibrated raster put 7 of those 11 in the zero bin (d9) and 4 in d1, with nothing in the top decile. The v3 raster moves all 11 out of d9: 2 land in d0 (Smartsville and Cherokee), 5 in d1, 3 in d2, and one outlier (Lake City) at d7. Median decile shifts from d9 to d1, top quintile from 5 of 13 to 7 of 11. The 500 m buffer plus the per-population feature stack do what they were supposed to do: the held-out Tertiary names are no longer landing on the v2 raster's zero pile.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_vs_v2_lindgren_decile_histogram.png)

**v3 closes the leak but doesn't fully erase it.** Buffering
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` to NaN within 1 km of any pit prevents the
trivial zero-distance shortcut, but SHAP TreeExplainer puts
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` at #1 in the top-5 feature ranking, 2.7x
the mean absolute SHAP value of the second feature (gravity).

![SHAP TreeExplainer ranks `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` as the dominant feature for the v3 Tertiary stack, 2.7x the mean absolute SHAP value of the next feature (`gravity`). The 1 km buffer pushes the trivial zero-distance shortcut out of reach, but the feature still encodes Tertiary belt membership; the ablation below measures how much of that lead is real geological signal vs residual shortcut the buffer admits.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_shap_top5_tertiary.png)

I ran an ablation to measure the residual leak: retrain Tertiary RF and XGB
without the feature entirely. RF dropped 10.5 percentage points (0.749 →
0.644) and XGB dropped 11.8 pp (0.826 → 0.708). About ten points of the v3
base AUCs are the buffered feature doing legitimate Tertiary-belt encoding
(cells in the broader hydraulic-mining belt have a real geological reason
to score higher) plus whatever residual shortcut the 1 km buffer still
admits.

![I dropped `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` from the v3 Tertiary feature stack and retrained the RF and XGB base learners on the same 20 spatial-block folds. RF AUC fell 10.5 percentage points (0.749 to 0.644); XGB fell 11.8 pp (0.826 to 0.708). About ten points of each model's baseline AUC was the buffered feature doing real Tertiary-belt encoding plus whatever residual shortcut the 1 km buffer admits; the remaining 0.64 to 0.71 AUC is what the other fifteen features carry on their own.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/v3_ablation_no_pit_proximity.png)

The Lindgren secondaries pass at 81.8% top quintile because they live in
the Tertiary belt by definition, so the belt-membership component of the
feature predicts them correctly even after the trivial zero-distance lookup
is blocked. v3.5 widens the buffer to 2 km to partition those ten points
between real signal and residual leak.

## v3.6: rasterizing the Tertiary labels

The v3 Tertiary label set was 158 cells, the centroid of each hydraulic
mine pit polygon rasterized to the 250 m grid. Each polygon contributes
exactly one positive even when it covers ten cells of bedrock. v3.6
rasterizes the full polygon: every cell whose 250 m square touches a
pit polygon counts as a positive, with a weight equal to the fractional
overlap (1.0 for interior cells, ~0.5 for boundary cells). The Tertiary
positive count grows from 158 binary cells to 2,709 weighted cells
(~17x). Quaternary labels are unchanged because Quaternary positives
were never the bottleneck.

The headline number jumps. The v3.6 Tertiary stacking out-of-fold AUC
lands at 0.993, up from v3's 0.970. The anchor-capture AUC-PA, the
metric the validation script reports as the headline, lands at 0.998
with a tight 95% confidence interval of [0.997, 0.998]. Both numbers
are mechanically eye-catching and methodologically suspicious. The
v3.6 audit (full F.2 suite, [model card](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/model_card_northern_sierra_placer.md))
unpacks why.

![Phase 1 knowledge-driven index vs. v3.6 Phase 2 calibrated models on the success-rate / Prediction-Area curve. The y-axis is the fraction of the seven anchor districts captured as a function of the fraction of area selected at the highest scores. Phase 2 fused and Phase 2 Tertiary land all seven anchors inside the top 1% of cells (capture 1.0 at 0.01 area). Phase 1 puts 2 of 7 in the top 1% and reaches all 7 by 5%. The Tertiary curve's near-perfect AUC-PA of 0.998 is partly because anchors are positive labels under the polygon-rasterization scheme; the buffered cells around each anchor centroid inherit the same positive label and the same neighborhood feature values. Quaternary (the unaffected population) sits at the more measured AUC-PA of 0.840 [0.759, 0.946].](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/success_rate_curve_phase1_vs_phase2.png)

Three audit findings together tell the polygon-rasterization story.
First, the SHAP TreeExplainer pass shows
`hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` dominating the Tertiary feature
ranking at 2.3x the mean absolute SHAP value of the next feature
(slope), with the v3 plan's expected top-5 feature recipe failing the
match gate at 2 of 5 (`paleochannel_likelihood` and
`tertiary_terrace_likelihood` did not make the v3.6 top-5 the way the
plan designed for). The model is leaning on pit-proximity harder in
v3.6 than it did in v3, which is what we would expect if the buffered
cells around each pit are pulling the gradient toward "score this
neighborhood high".

Second, the Lindgren PP73 secondary-district blind set tells a more
measured story. Of 350 truly blind hydraulic-mining sites (456
secondaries minus 106 deduplicated against pit polygons, anchor
districts, and MRDS records inside a 500 m buffer), the v3.6 fused
calibrated score puts 28% in its top decile, against a knowledge-driven
Phase 1 baseline of 22% and a random baseline of 10%. The fused model
adds 6 percentage points of top-decile capture over the Phase 1
baseline. Real signal, modest. Not the eye-popping 0.998 the headline
number suggests.

Third, the north-south spatial-cross-validation pass trains on the
southern half of the AOI (zero anchor districts in training, 68
Tertiary positives + 320 Quaternary positives) and predicts on the
northern half (where all 7 anchors sit). The south-trained model still
captures 7 of 7 Tertiary anchors at the top 5% and 3 of 7 Quaternary
anchors at the top decile, matching the in-sample baseline at delta
zero. The model generalizes spatially without anchor leakage. That
result is independent of polygon-rasterization and confirms the
underlying geomorphic signal is real, even if the headline OOF AUC
overstates how much of it the model is capturing.

The net read on v3.6 is two parts. The Quaternary stack at AUC-PA
0.840 [0.759, 0.946] is healthy: nothing changed between v3 and v3.6
on the Quaternary side, the metric matches v3's 0.832, and the SHAP
top-5 leans on elevation, distance-to-lode, topographic position, and
gravity. The Tertiary stack at AUC-PA 0.998 is partly inflated by
polygon-rasterization label propagation; the leak-free number is
probably closer to v3's 0.97 or below, and the [Open validation
questions](#open-validation-questions) section below lists the
audits that would partition the signal cleanly. The fused deliverable
goldbug consumes is still the right thing to ship for per-claim
ranking because (a) the calibrated probability scale is preserved
(Brier 0.0025 on the calibrated raster, post-isotonic ECE = 0.004 in
the audit), and (b) goldbug uses the score as a within-AOI ranking
rather than as an absolute probability claim, so the metric inflation
matters less for its consumer pattern.

## MAF anomaly detection on Au pathfinder geochem

I ported the Scheidt, Mathieu, Yin, Wang, Caers (2024)
[Masked Autoregressive Flow framework](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11053-024-10409-2)
for geochemical anomaly detection to the Northern Sierra placer AOI, with
Au pathfinder elements (As, Sb, Bi, W, Cu) substituted for the original
paper's Li-Cs-Ta pegmatite chemistry. The MAF normalizing flow learns
the joint distribution of pathfinder values across NGDB stream-sediment
samples; samples with low log-density under the fitted flow are flagged
as candidate anomalies. The Scheidt paper uses this to surface
LCT-pegmatite candidates from continental-scale databases. Here it
surfaces samples whose 5-element pathfinder fingerprint deviates from
the regional baseline.

The training set is 482 NGDB samples with complete-case measurements
across all 5 pathfinder elements. That is much smaller than Scheidt's
~10k pegmatite-region samples, so the fitted MAF density is noisier
than the paper's; the relative anomaly ranking holds but absolute
scores should not be over-interpreted. Median anomaly score lands at
5.6 (in nats), p90 at 8.9, p99 at 13.3, max at 22.2, a long-tail
distribution where a small set of strongly anomalous samples stands
out cleanly from the bulk.

![Per-sample MAF anomaly score over the Northern Sierra NGDB stream-sediment AOI (482 samples × 5 elements: As, Sb, Bi, W, Cu). Color is the anomaly score (negative log-density) clipped at the p99; white-circled samples are the top decile (n=49). The strongest anomalies cluster around the Tertiary deep-gravel belt south of Forest Hill and along the Yuba and American river systems. Method follows Scheidt et al. 2024, DOI 10.1007/s11053-024-10409-2.](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/fig_maf_anomaly_map.png)

The per-sample anomaly scores are exported to
`maf_geochem_anomaly_scores.parquet` for downstream use. In v3.5.LF, the
MAF score becomes a `geochem_maf_anomaly` feature on the 250 m grid
via inverse-distance interpolation from the 482 sample points; the
ablation will measure how much it adds over the current Hawkes-Au
catchment feature.

## Monte Carlo uncertainty bracket on the Quaternary raster

The headline calibrated Quaternary raster reports a single per-cell
probability. That hides the model's own uncertainty about negatives:
30 Mordelet-Vert PU-bagging classifiers each see a different random
"unlabeled-as-negative" subsample, and their predictions at any given
cell sample from the epistemic uncertainty over which unlabeled cells
are actually negative. The quantiles across that sample form a
per-cell confidence band, following the entry-level Monte
Carlo precursor to the full Bayesian Evidential Learning framework
in [Yin, Strebelle, Caers (2020)](https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-13-651-2020).

I aggregate the 30 per-bag predictions into P05 / P50 / P95 per cell
and report the ±90% spread (P95 − P05) as the per-cell uncertainty
metric. Across the AOI:

- Spread median: 0.050  (most cells have a ±5% probability band)
- Spread p90:    0.111
- Spread p99:    0.139
- Spread max:    0.201

![PU-bagging median probability (left) vs ±90% Monte Carlo uncertainty bracket (right) for the v3 Quaternary model on the Northern Sierra AOI. The point estimate shows the expected stream-network pattern: yellow streaks along the modern channel system flanked by darker negatives. The uncertainty bracket is narrow over both clearly-positive (channel) and clearly-negative (upland) regions, and widest in the boundary zones, exactly where PU bagging is by design most informative. Reporting in P05/P50/P95 instead of a single point estimate gives goldbug a per-claim confidence interval to act on; a narrow bracket means "the 30 base learners all agree this cell scores high (or low)," a wide bracket means "the model is unsure even after pooling 30 bag predictions, treat the score as advisory."](data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/fig_mc_uncertainty_bracket.png)

The triplet of GeoTIFFs ships as
`prospectivity_placer_quaternary_250m_mc_{p05,p50,p95,spread}_{3310,4326}.tif`.
goldbug consumes them via the same zonal-mean machinery as the
calibrated raster, producing per-claim P05/P50/P95 sampled against the
parcel footprint.

## What this doesn't prove

- **The hot cells contain extractable gold.** Hydraulic-pit polygons mark
  where 1850s-1884 miners chose to wash hillsides, not where commercial pay
  exists today. The calibrated probability is "this cell looks like a
  Tertiary-deep-gravel or Quaternary-modern-channel placer setting," not
  "you will find gold here at depth X with thickness Y."
- **The model transfers off the AOI.** A Calaveras County transfer test
  (same Mother Lode trend, different watersheds) is queued. Expect a drop
  comparable to the Klamath-region transfer on the lode side (Mother Lode →
  Klamath went from AUC 0.85 to 0.58). Within-AOI generalization is the
  story so far.
- **The Tertiary v3.6 anchor-capture AUC-PA of 0.998 (and stacking
  out-of-fold AUC of 0.993) is the headline number.** The polygon-rasterization
  jump from 158 binary centroid cells to 2,709 weighted cells inflates
  both metrics: buffered cells around each pit centroid inherit the same
  positive label and the same neighborhood feature values, so the model
  is partly memorizing pit polygons rather than learning placer
  geomorphology. The pit-proximity ablation against v3 measured about
  ten percentage points of base AUC coming from
  `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered`; v3.6's SHAP shows the model
  leaning on that feature even harder than v3 did (2.3x the next
  feature's mean absolute SHAP value). The Lindgren PP73 secondary
  blind set adds 6 percentage points of top-decile capture over the
  knowledge-driven Phase 1 baseline, which is the trustworthy lower
  bound on real signal over the geologist-set index. The leak-free
  number is probably in the 0.85-0.97 band, closer to v3's 0.97 than
  to v3.6's 0.998.
- **The Phase 1 anchor gate is a sufficient external test.** Lindgren PP73
  secondaries are the better test, because they were never seen by any
  classifier or scorer and they are 500 m de-duped from any training pit
  polygon. v3 passes Lindgren at 81.8% top quintile; v2 did not.

## Open validation questions

These are the audits I haven't run yet that would harden the v3.6
result if a reviewer asked, ordered roughly by what closes the
biggest unanswered concern first.

- **Whether the v3.6 polygon-rasterization actually inflates the OOF AUC, or genuinely adds signal:** retrain v3.6 with centroid-only positive labels (drop the buffer-cell expansion that grew Tertiary positives from 158 binary cells to 2,709 weighted cells), keep the same stacked pipeline (RF + LGBM + XGB into a logistic-regression meta-learner, isotonic calibration, 20 km spatial-block CV), compare the resulting stacking OOF AUC to v3.6's 0.993. If centroid-only lands near v3's 0.970 the buffer expansion is incremental signal; a sharp drop would say the 0.993 was label-propagation inflation.
- **Whether the dominant `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m_buffered` feature carries disproportionate weight on v3.6 specifically:** rerun `scripts/v3_ablation_no_pit_proximity.py` against the v3.6 stacking ensemble, not just the v3 RF + XGB pair from the June 3 ablation. The v3 result showed 10.5 pp RF drop and 11.8 pp XGB drop when the feature was removed (intermediate range; neither pure incremental signal nor pure leakage shortcut). A much larger drop on v3.6 would imply the polygon-rasterization made the model lean harder on the feature.
- **Whether the model generalizes across the Sierra belt, not just inside Northern Sierra:** run `scripts/northern_sierra_placer_calaveras_transfer.py` (scripted but not yet executed) to train on Northern Sierra v3.6 calibrated labels and score the Mother Lode / Calaveras County AOI cell-for-cell. Compare to the Mother Lode v3 stacking OOF (0.85) for a within-province transfer-loss number; the Klamath cross-province test scored 0.58 on the lode side. Calaveras is the same orogenic-Au belt but a different watershed system.
- **Whether v3.6 stays calibrated at the operational top-X% probability bins goldbug actually consumes:** stratified Brier score and per-bin ECE at the top 1% / 5% / 10% probability quantiles separately per population, instead of the overall ECE we already report (Tertiary post-calibration 0.004, Quaternary post-calibration 0.000). Overall ECE can mask tail mis-calibration where the highest-confidence predictions diverge from the observed positive rate.
- **Whether the per-fold Hawkes refold (`refold_hawkes=True`) actually prevents the contagion leakage it was designed to prevent:** ablate to `refold_hawkes=False` on v3.6 Tertiary, compare per-fold AUC distributions and SHAP attributions on `catchment_au_hawkes`. If the refold is doing its job, refold-off should show higher in-fold AUC (leakage helps inside the fold) but worse Lindgren PP73 blind-set top-decile capture (true generalization gets worse when the leakage path is open).
- **Whether channel-scale features computed on a finer DEM than the current 30 m paleochannel composite would close the v3.6 Quaternary gap:** the v3 paleochannel-likelihood feature uses a 30 m internal resolution downsampled from native 1 m 3DEP LIDAR; the other DEM derivatives (slope, TPI, TWI, ksn) are computed on a 90 m baseline. Five queued channel-scale features need 10 m DEM input to encode anything new: chi-mapping knickpoint indicator (Mudd 2014 channel-steepness method, picks up bedrock-confined reaches), paleochannel-confluence proximity (where two paleo-flow paths join), bedrock-confined NHD reaches (where modern channels intersect mapped bedrock outcrop), NHDPlus VAA connectivity (the value-added-attribute network distance to a known placer record along the flow graph), and along-channel gradient discontinuity (where slope changes mid-reach, the classic placer-trap site). All are tiled-compute jobs that produce a single per-cell scalar at 250 m grid resolution; they go into placer-v3.8.0. The model itself stays on the 250 m training grid because 10 m would be ~625× more cells, which pushes per-fold training from ~14 hours to weeks.

## Where to look

The deliverable rasters live at
`data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/prospectivity_placer_placer_tertiary_250m_calibrated_{3310,4326}.tif`
and the matching Quaternary pair when the v3 Quaternary pipeline finishes.
[goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/) ingests the fused raster via
its zonal-mean machinery: per-claim mean + max + count + has-data flag
against the calibrated probability, weighted by the claim polygon
footprint. No API contract beyond the GeoTIFF.

For the full data + model walkthrough (input layer maps, the v2-to-v3
feature redesign, the ablation measurement chart, the leakage audit
traces), see the
[data + model overview notebook](notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/data_overview.html).
That notebook consolidates what previously lived across an internal
data-exploration walkthrough and five v3-phase review notebooks.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
